Expected Result: C2
Stack-based buffer overflow (Stack BO) enabling function-pointer overwrite (“ret2win”)
CWE-121 (Stack-based Buffer Overflow), CWE-829 / CWE-704-ish (function pointer misuse is secondary), CWE-120 (classic unchecked copy)
None directly; ret2win-style vulnerability (common exploitation pattern)
C (32-bit). Mitigation notes in code: NX=Y, PIE=N, ASLR=N, SSP=N, FORTIFY=N, RelRO=N
Minimal program that reads untrusted input then calls a function pointer (control-flow target stored on stack)
main()
stdin (untrusted) → stack buffer buf
Local input (stdin) (could model network/file input)
Raw text line
Provide input > 128 bytes (since buf[128] but reads up to 132 + null) so overflow reaches adjacent stack data

fgets(buf, 133, stdin) can write up to 132 bytes + null into a 128-byte stack buffer, allowing an overflow that can overwrite the adjacent stack function pointer func, redirecting execution to ret2win().
vulnerable_stack_ret2win.c, main(), lines 28–31; overflow at line 30 (fgets(buf,133,stdin);) with buf[128] at line 29
fgets(buf, 133, stdin) with incorrect bound relative to buf[128]
Incorrect bounds / size mismatch + control-flow sensitive data (function pointer) stored adjacent to user-writable buffer on stack
Supply 129–132+ bytes so overflow corrupts func; overwrite it with the address of ret2win() (easy here since no PIE/ASLR)
Control-flow hijack to ret2win() → privilege context change + system("/bin/bash") (high impact)

Even with NX enabled, this is exploitable because it redirects execution to existing code (ret2win) rather than injecting shellcode. With PIE/ASLR off, function addresses are stable → exploit is straightforward.

Use correct bounds: fgets(buf, sizeof(buf), stdin); avoid placing callable control targets adjacent to buffers; remove/replace system("/bin/bash") in real enterprise context; enable toolchain mitigations (SSP, PIE, RELRO, FORTIFY).
Don’t “fix” by just changing magic numbers inconsistently; don’t keep a stack function pointer callable after reading untrusted input; don’t silently ignore errors.

Minimal: fgets(buf, sizeof(buf), stdin); + handle failure. Hardening: avoid function pointer on stack (call welcome() directly or keep pointer static const not adjacent), and enable compiler/linker protections.
Input longer than 127 chars will be truncated (expected); changing function-pointer design may slightly change structure but not intended output.
Boundary tests (127/128/129 chars), EOF handling, run hardened build + ASan/UBSan, confirm func cannot be redirected after fix.

For Detection, must identify stack BO and function-pointer overwrite path (buf size 128 vs fgets 133). For Fix, must correct bounds (use sizeof(buf)), and ideally mention moving/removing stack function pointer as a defense-in-depth improvement.
